title: Umbilical Cord Prolapse in a Laboring 30-Year-Old G3P2 at Term Immediately After Spontaneous Rupture of Membranes with a High, Unengaged Fetal Head: Recognizing Sudden Fetal Bradycardia + a Palpable Pulsating Cord as an Obstetric Emergency, Relieving Cord Compression (Manually Elevating the Presenting Part + Maternal Positioning), and LEADING the Emergency Team to an Immediate Cesarean
subtitle: UMBILICAL CORD PROLAPSE = the umbilical cord slips below or alongside the fetal presenting part after the membranes rupture, so the cord becomes COMPRESSED between the presenting part and the maternal pelvis — rapidly impairing fetal oxygenation and causing fetal hypoxia/bradycardia; it is an acute OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY requiring an immediate, coordinated team response and expedited delivery. TYPES: OVERT (the cord protrudes through the cervix into/out of the vagina, often visible or palpable) vs OCCULT (the cord lies alongside the presenting part and is not palpable — suspected from the FHR pattern). RISK FACTORS center on a presenting part that does NOT fill the pelvic inlet (so the cord can slip past) and on membrane rupture: a HIGH/UNENGAGED presenting part at rupture, MALPRESENTATION (breech, transverse/oblique lie, footling), POLYHYDRAMNIOS (large gush of fluid sweeps the cord down), PREMATURITY/small fetus, MULTIPARITY, MULTIPLE GESTATION, a long umbilical cord, and ARTIFICIAL RUPTURE OF MEMBRANES (AROM) performed when the presenting part is high/unengaged (so AROM is avoided/done cautiously in that setting). RECOGNITION: a SUDDEN, PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA or new moderate-to-severe VARIABLE DECELERATIONS occurring IMMEDIATELY after rupture of membranes is cord prolapse until proven otherwise → perform a STERILE VAGINAL EXAM, which may reveal a PALPABLE (often PULSATING) cord, or the cord may be VISIBLE at/protruding from the introitus (overt). IMMEDIATE INTERVENTIONS — the goal is to RELIEVE CORD COMPRESSION and EXPEDITE DELIVERY, and the classic high-yield sequence is: (1) CALL FOR HELP / activate the obstetric emergency team immediately (notify the obstetrician/midwife, anesthesia, neonatal/NICU team, and charge nurse; mobilize the OR) — this is fundamentally a leadership-and-delegation emergency; (2) MANUALLY ELEVATE THE PRESENTING PART off the cord — the examiner inserts a gloved hand into the vagina and pushes the presenting part UPWARD (cephalad) and HOLDS it off the cord, and DOES NOT REMOVE the hand until delivery (typically maintaining elevation continuously, including during transfer to the OR and until the cesarean delivers the fetus); (3) REPOSITION THE MOTHER to use gravity to relieve cord pressure — KNEE-CHEST (knee-to-chest) position, steep TRENDELENBURG, or exaggerated Sims/elevated-hips position; (4) give the mother high-flow OXYGEN, ensure IV access/fluids, STOP oxytocin (and consider a tocolytic to reduce contractions), and maintain CONTINUOUS FETAL HEART RATE monitoring; (5) if the cord is PROTRUDING/exposed, do NOT attempt to push it back into the uterus, MINIMIZE HANDLING of the cord (handling/cold cause vasospasm), and keep it warm and moist (cover with warm sterile saline-soaked gauze); (6) PREPARE FOR AN EMERGENT (STAT) CESAREAN DELIVERY — the definitive treatment — unless a spontaneous vaginal delivery is truly imminent and faster; an alternative temporizing maneuver to relieve pressure (especially if continuous manual elevation during transport is difficult) is BLADDER FILLING (instilling ~500 mL of saline into the bladder via an indwelling catheter to lift the presenting part). NEONATAL preparation: the fetus may be hypoxic/acidotic, so have the neonatal team ready for RESUSCITATION (NRP) at delivery. LEADERSHIP/DELEGATION + TEAMWORK are decisive (and the focus of this case): the responding nurse must instantly RECOGNIZE the emergency, CALL IT OUT and activate the team, and DELEGATE clear roles with closed-loop communication — one clinician keeps a gloved hand elevating the presenting part and stays with the patient (and accompanies her to the OR maintaining elevation), while others notify/mobilize the OB + anesthesia + neonatal team + OR, position the mother (knee-chest/Trendelenburg), apply oxygen + IV fluids + stop oxytocin, monitor the FHR, obtain consent/prep for cesarean, and DOCUMENT times — using SBAR for concise handoffs. PREVENTION/AWARENESS: assess the FHR IMMEDIATELY after any rupture of membranes (spontaneous or artificial), recognize high-risk situations (unengaged/high presenting part, malpresentation, polyhydramnios), and avoid/limit AROM when the presenting part is not engaged. NURSING ROLE: recognize cord prolapse the instant the FHR drops after ROM, relieve cord compression (manual elevation + maternal positioning) without delay, lead and delegate the coordinated emergency response, and expedite the patient to a STAT cesarean with the neonatal team ready — all while supporting the frightened mother/family

patient.name: T. Okafor
patient.code: Full code; laboring patient + fetus (two patients); husband at bedside; goal = immediately relieve cord compression and expedite delivery to prevent fetal hypoxic injury, with a coordinated emergency team response
patient.admitDate: In active labor at term (G3P2) with the membranes intact and the fetal head still HIGH/UNENGAGED. Moments ago she had a SPONTANEOUS RUPTURE OF MEMBRANES with a large gush of fluid, immediately followed by a SUDDEN PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA (FHR ~70 bpm). The nurse performed a sterile vaginal exam and PALPATED A PULSATING UMBILICAL CORD below the presenting part — UMBILICAL CORD PROLAPSE, an obstetric emergency. The nurse must relieve cord compression and lead an immediate, coordinated team response toward a STAT cesarean.
patient.room: Labor & Delivery; continuous electronic fetal monitoring; OR + anesthesia + neonatal team being mobilized; husband at bedside; obstetric emergency response activated

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; FETAL HEART RATE drops SUDDENLY to ~70 bpm (prolonged bradycardia) right after a large gush of fluid with the fetal head still high/unengaged; mother anxious; this abrupt post-ROM bradycardia is cord prolapse until proven otherwise

37.0°C; sterile vaginal exam reveals a PALPABLE, PULSATING umbilical cord below the presenting part = OVERT UMBILICAL CORD PROLAPSE; FHR remains bradycardic with compression → CALL FOR HELP + relieve cord compression immediately

37.0°C; examiner's gloved hand MANUALLY ELEVATES the presenting part OFF the cord (kept in place); mother placed in KNEE-CHEST/Trendelenburg; high-flow oxygen + IV fluids; oxytocin STOPPED; continuous FHR monitoring; obstetric emergency team activated (OB, anesthesia, neonatal/NICU, charge nurse, OR) with closed-loop role assignment; with compression relieved, FHR begins to recover toward 110-120

vitals.time: Transfer to OR for STAT cesarean (elevation maintained)
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; the clinician maintaining manual elevation of the presenting part ACCOMPANIES the patient to the OR WITHOUT removing the hand; exposed cord (if protruding) kept warm + moist with saline gauze and NOT pushed back or handled excessively; STAT cesarean underway with neonatal team ready for resuscitation; FHR maintained with compression relieved

37.0°C; emergent cesarean delivery completed; NEWBORN required brief PPV per NRP then vigorous, Apgars improving (e.g., 5 → 8 → 9), cord gases reflecting transient compression but improving; rapid recognition + sustained cord-compression relief preserved fetal oxygenation until delivery; mother stable; family updated

37.0°C; mother recovering well post-cesarean; newborn stable in the nursery/NICU as indicated; team DEBRIEF conducted (what went well: rapid recognition, immediate cord-compression relief, coordinated team response); emotional support for the frightened parents; documentation of times/interventions completed

labs.v: 30 y/o woman T. Okafor, G3P2, in active labor at term, with the fetal head still HIGH and UNENGAGED. She had a SPONTANEOUS RUPTURE OF MEMBRANES with a large gush of fluid, IMMEDIATELY followed by a SUDDEN, PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA (FHR ~70 bpm). A new, abrupt fetal bradycardia (or new moderate-severe variable decelerations) occurring right after rupture of membranes is UMBILICAL CORD PROLAPSE until proven otherwise. The nurse immediately performed a STERILE VAGINAL EXAM and PALPATED a PULSATING umbilical cord below the presenting part — confirming OVERT UMBILICAL CORD PROLAPSE, an obstetric EMERGENCY in which the cord is compressed between the presenting part and the maternal pelvis, rapidly impairing fetal oxygenation. RISK FACTORS present here: a HIGH/UNENGAGED presenting part at the time of rupture (the classic setup — the cord can slip past a presenting part that doesn't fill the pelvic inlet) and the large fluid gush; other risk factors include malpresentation (breech/transverse), polyhydramnios, prematurity/small fetus, multiparity, multiple gestation, a long cord, and AROM with a high presenting part. PMH/OB: G3P2, term, uncomplicated pregnancy until now; in active labor. SOCIAL: husband at bedside, frightened; supportive. The fetus is now at risk of hypoxic injury from cord compression, and minutes matter. The nurse's immediate priorities: relieve cord compression, activate and lead the emergency team, maintain fetal oxygenation, and expedite delivery (STAT cesarean) — while supporting the terrified parents. This is both a clinical emergency and a LEADERSHIP/DELEGATION challenge requiring instant recognition, calling for help, and coordinated role assignment.
labs.r: Sudden prolonged fetal bradycardia (or new severe variable decels) immediately after ROM = umbilical cord prolapse until proven otherwise → sterile vaginal exam may reveal a palpable/pulsating or visible cord (overt). Risk: high/unengaged presenting part at rupture, malpresentation, polyhydramnios, prematurity, AROM with high presenting part. RN role: instant recognition + relieve compression + activate/lead the team + expedite delivery.

labs.v: The IMMEDIATE goal in cord prolapse is to RELIEVE CORD COMPRESSION (restore fetal oxygenation) while EXPEDITING DELIVERY; the classic, high-yield emergency sequence: (1) CALL FOR HELP / ACTIVATE THE OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY TEAM instantly — summon the obstetrician/midwife, anesthesia, the neonatal/NICU team, the charge nurse, and mobilize the OR; do not leave the patient, and do not attempt to manage it alone. (2) MANUALLY ELEVATE THE PRESENTING PART off the cord — insert a gloved hand into the vagina and push the presenting part UPWARD (cephalad), lifting it OFF the cord to relieve compression, and HOLD it there; CRITICALLY, do NOT remove the hand — maintain continuous elevation through preparation, transport to the OR, and until the fetus is delivered by cesarean. (3) REPOSITION THE MOTHER to use gravity: KNEE-CHEST (knee-to-chest) position, steep TRENDELENBURG, or an exaggerated Sims/elevated-hips position — these shift the presenting part off the cord. (4) Apply high-flow maternal OXYGEN (e.g., 8-10 L/min non-rebreather), ensure IV access + fluids, STOP oxytocin (reduces contraction-driven compression), consider a TOCOLYTIC (e.g., terbutaline) to relax the uterus, and maintain CONTINUOUS FHR monitoring to track fetal status. (5) If the cord is PROTRUDING/exposed: do NOT attempt to push it back into the uterus or replace it; MINIMIZE HANDLING (manipulation and cold trigger umbilical-vessel VASOSPASM, worsening fetal hypoxia); keep it WARM and MOIST by covering it with warm STERILE SALINE-soaked gauze. (6) PREPARE FOR EMERGENT (STAT) CESAREAN delivery — the definitive treatment — unless a spontaneous vaginal delivery is genuinely imminent and would be faster (e.g., complete dilation + low station). (7) An alternative temporizing maneuver, especially if maintaining continuous manual elevation during transport is impractical, is BLADDER FILLING — instilling ~500 mL of sterile saline into the bladder through an indwelling catheter to elevate the presenting part off the cord. NEONATAL prep: the fetus may be hypoxic/acidotic — ensure the neonatal team is present and ready for RESUSCITATION (NRP) at delivery. The single most important bedside action the moment prolapse is identified is to GET THE PRESENTING PART OFF THE CORD (manual elevation + positioning) and KEEP IT OFF until delivery, while the team mobilizes for cesarean.
labs.r: Relieve cord compression + expedite delivery: (1) CALL FOR HELP/activate the team; (2) MANUALLY ELEVATE the presenting part off the cord and DON'T remove the hand until delivery; (3) position knee-chest/Trendelenburg; (4) high-flow O2 + IV fluids + STOP oxytocin ± tocolytic + continuous FHR; (5) if cord exposed: don't push it back, minimize handling, keep warm/moist with saline gauze; (6) STAT cesarean (unless vaginal delivery truly imminent); (7) bladder filling as an alternative to elevate. Neonatal team ready (NRP). RN role: get + keep the presenting part off the cord.

labs.v: Cord prolapse is a paradigm 'call it out and run the room' emergency, and outcomes hinge on LEADERSHIP, DELEGATION, and TEAMWORK. The responding nurse must: (1) RECOGNIZE the emergency instantly (post-ROM bradycardia → exam → prolapse) and immediately CALL FOR HELP/activate the obstetric emergency (rapid-response) team rather than trying to manage alone; (2) ASSIGN CLEAR ROLES with CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION as people arrive, because many time-critical tasks must happen simultaneously: ONE clinician keeps a gloved hand elevating the presenting part off the cord and STAYS WITH THE PATIENT (and accompanies her to the OR without releasing the elevation); others NOTIFY/mobilize the OBSTETRICIAN, ANESTHESIA, NEONATAL/NICU team, and the OR; POSITION the mother (knee-chest/Trendelenburg); apply OXYGEN + IV fluids + STOP oxytocin; MONITOR the FHR; obtain/prepare CONSENT and surgical prep for the cesarean; manage the cord if exposed (warm saline gauze, minimal handling); SUPPORT the frightened patient/partner; and DOCUMENT the timeline (time of ROM, FHR change, recognition, interventions, decision-to-incision, delivery). (3) Communicate using SBAR for concise handoffs to each responder and to the OR/anesthesia/neonatal teams (Situation: cord prolapse with fetal bradycardia; Background: G3P2 term, ROM with unengaged head; Assessment: cord palpated/pulsating, presenting part being elevated, FHR recovering with elevation; Recommendation: STAT cesarean, team and OR ready). (4) DELEGATE by scope: unlicensed assistive personnel and other staff can fetch equipment, move the bed/transport, set up the OR, and run for supplies, while RN/provider-level tasks (the vaginal exam + manual elevation, FHR interpretation, medication administration) remain with licensed staff; the person elevating the presenting part should be the one continuously responsible for that task through delivery. (5) Maintain SITUATIONAL leadership — keep the team focused, avoid duplicated/missed tasks, and ensure the patient is moved to the OR rapidly (the 'decision-to-incision' interval matters). (6) After the emergency, lead a DEBRIEF (what went well, opportunities) and support the team and family. This coordinated, role-clear, closed-loop response — built on instant recognition and continuous cord-compression relief — is what gets a salvageable fetus delivered in time.
labs.r: Leadership/delegation in cord prolapse: instantly recognize + CALL FOR HELP/activate the team (don't manage alone); assign clear roles with CLOSED-LOOP communication (one person continuously elevates the presenting part + stays with the patient → to OR; others mobilize OB/anesthesia/neonatal/OR, position mother, O2/IV/stop oxytocin, monitor FHR, prep consent/cesarean, support family, document times); SBAR handoffs; delegate by scope (UAP fetch/transport/setup; licensed = exam/elevation/FHR/meds); debrief after. RN role: lead + delegate the coordinated response.

labs.n: Prevention/awareness, neonatal care, and Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PREVENTION/AWARENESS: cord prolapse cannot always be prevented, but risk is reduced and detection hastened by — assessing the FETAL HEART RATE IMMEDIATELY after ANY rupture of membranes (spontaneous or artificial) and for several minutes after; recognizing HIGH-RISK situations (a HIGH/UNENGAGED presenting part, malpresentation such as breech/transverse, polyhydramnios, prematurity, multiple gestation) and maintaining heightened vigilance; AVOIDING or cautiously performing ARTIFICIAL RUPTURE OF MEMBRANES (AROM) when the presenting part is NOT engaged (and controlled AROM with fundal/suprapubic pressure to keep the head applied if AROM is necessary); and considering bed rest/positioning for an unengaged presenting part with ruptured membranes. NEONATAL CARE: because the fetus endures cord compression and possible hypoxia/acidosis, the NEONATAL team must be present and ready for RESUSCITATION (NRP — warm/dry/stimulate, assess, positive-pressure ventilation, and further steps as needed), and cord blood gases are obtained; outcomes are usually good when prolapse is recognized immediately and compression is relieved and delivery expedited. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct emergency practice): the nurse did NOT delay or leave the patient to 'go get' someone alone (called for help while staying + relieving compression); did NOT remove the elevating hand before delivery; did NOT attempt to PUSH the prolapsed cord back into the uterus or handle it excessively (which causes vasospasm); did NOT continue oxytocin (it was stopped); did NOT simply continue pushing for a vaginal delivery when the head was high (a STAT cesarean was mobilized); and did NOT lay the mother flat/supine without elevating the presenting part (knee-chest/Trendelenburg used). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: instant RECOGNITION of post-ROM bradycardia as cord prolapse + immediate sterile exam; immediate and CONTINUOUS cord-compression relief (manual elevation + maternal positioning) maintained through transport to the OR; rapid activation + coordinated leadership of the emergency team with clear role delegation + SBAR; high-flow oxygen + stopped oxytocin + continuous FHR; a STAT cesarean with the neonatal team ready; and supportive care + debrief. EXPECTED outcome (as here): with rapid recognition, sustained compression relief, and an expedited cesarean, fetal oxygenation is preserved until delivery and the newborn — though it may need brief resuscitation — does well. The case underscores that cord prolapse is won or lost in the first minutes by RECOGNIZING it, RELIEVING the compression and keeping it relieved, and LEADING a fast, coordinated team to delivery.
labs.r: Prevention/awareness: assess FHR immediately after ANY ROM; vigilance with high/unengaged presenting part/malpresentation/polyhydramnios; avoid/cautious AROM if presenting part unengaged. Neonatal team ready for NRP + cord gases. Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't leave patient, didn't remove the elevating hand, didn't push cord back/handle it, stopped oxytocin, mobilized STAT cesarean, used knee-chest not flat). Protective: instant recognition + sustained compression relief + coordinated team leadership + STAT cesarean. RN role: recognize + relieve + lead → delivery.

nursesNote: Labor & Delivery — 30 y/o G3P2 at term in active labor with the fetal head still HIGH/UNENGAGED. SPONTANEOUS ROM with a large gush of fluid → IMMEDIATELY followed by SUDDEN PROLONGED FETAL BRADYCARDIA (FHR ~70). (New abrupt bradycardia right after ROM = cord prolapse until proven otherwise.) Performed STERILE VAGINAL EXAM → PALPATED a PULSATING umbilical cord below the presenting part = OVERT UMBILICAL CORD PROLAPSE (obstetric EMERGENCY; cord compressed between presenting part + pelvis → fetal hypoxia). Risk factors present: high/unengaged presenting part at rupture + large fluid gush. Husband at bedside, frightened. IMMEDIATE RESPONSE (relieve compression + expedite delivery + lead the team): (1) CALLED FOR HELP / activated the obstetric emergency team (OB, anesthesia, neonatal/NICU, charge nurse, OR) WITHOUT leaving the patient; (2) examiner's gloved hand MANUALLY ELEVATED the presenting part OFF the cord and KEPT it there (did NOT remove the hand); (3) positioned mother KNEE-CHEST/Trendelenburg; (4) high-flow O2 (SpO2 100%), IV fluids, STOPPED oxytocin (± tocolytic considered), CONTINUOUS FHR monitoring; (5) (cord at introitus) did NOT push it back, MINIMIZED handling (vasospasm), kept warm + moist with sterile saline gauze; (6) mobilized STAT CESAREAN (head high → not a candidate for immediate vaginal delivery). With compression relieved, FHR recovered toward 110-120. LEADERSHIP/DELEGATION: assigned clear roles with CLOSED-LOOP communication — one clinician continuously elevates the presenting part + stays with the patient (and rode to the OR maintaining elevation); others notified/mobilized OB + anesthesia + neonatal + OR, positioned the mother, ran O2/IV/stopped oxytocin, monitored FHR, prepped consent + cesarean, supported the partner, and documented the timeline (ROM time, FHR change, recognition, interventions, decision-to-incision, delivery); SBAR handoffs to OR/anesthesia/neonatal. TRANSFER TO OR: elevation maintained continuously; exposed cord kept warm/moist + not handled; STAT cesarean with neonatal team ready for NRP. DELIVERY: emergent cesarean completed; NEWBORN needed brief PPV per NRP then vigorous (Apgars ~5 → 8 → 9; cord gases reflecting transient compression, improving) — rapid recognition + sustained compression relief preserved oxygenation until delivery; mother stable. POSTPARTUM: mother recovering well; newborn stable; team DEBRIEF (rapid recognition + immediate continuous compression relief + coordinated response); emotional support for parents; documentation complete. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did not leave the patient/delay, did not remove the elevating hand before delivery, did not push the cord back or handle it excessively, stopped oxytocin, mobilized STAT cesarean, used knee-chest (not flat supine).
providerOrders: OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY: umbilical cord prolapse with fetal bradycardia — activate the obstetric rapid-response/emergency team immediately
DIAGNOSIS: overt umbilical cord prolapse (palpable pulsating cord) after spontaneous ROM with a high/unengaged presenting part; fetal bradycardia
CODE STATUS: full code; two patients (mother + fetus); expedite delivery
RELIEVE CORD COMPRESSION (immediately + continuously):
Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord with a gloved hand and DO NOT remove it until delivery (maintain through transport to the OR)
Position the mother knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg (or exaggerated Sims/elevated hips); do NOT lay her flat without elevating the presenting part
If the cord is protruding/exposed: do NOT push it back into the uterus; minimize handling (prevents vasospasm); keep it warm and moist with sterile saline-soaked gauze
SUPPORT FETAL OXYGENATION:
High-flow maternal oxygen (8-10 L/min non-rebreather); IV access + fluid bolus
STOP oxytocin; consider a tocolytic (e.g., terbutaline) to reduce contractions
Continuous electronic fetal heart rate monitoring
EXPEDITE DELIVERY:
Prepare for STAT cesarean delivery (definitive treatment) — mobilize OR, anesthesia, and surgical team; obtain emergency consent; minimize decision-to-incision time
Proceed to immediate vaginal delivery ONLY if it is genuinely imminent and faster (complete dilation + low station)
Alternative temporizing maneuver if continuous manual elevation during transport is impractical: bladder filling (~500 mL sterile saline via indwelling catheter) to elevate the presenting part
TEAM LEADERSHIP / DELEGATION / COMMUNICATION:
Call for help / activate the team immediately; do not leave the patient or manage alone

Assign clear roles with closed-loop communication: one clinician maintains presenting-part elevation + stays with the patient (to OR); others mobilize OB/anesthesia/neonatal/OR, position the mother, give O2/IV/stop oxytocin, monitor FHR, prep consent/cesarean, support the family, and document times
SBAR handoffs to the OR, anesthesia, and neonatal teams; delegate by scope (UAP/support: fetch/transport/OR setup; licensed: exam/elevation/FHR/meds)
Document the timeline: ROM time, FHR change, recognition, interventions, decision-to-incision, and delivery
NEONATAL PREPARATION:
Neonatal/NICU team present and ready for resuscitation (NRP) at delivery; obtain cord blood gases
MATERNAL/FAMILY SUPPORT + AFTERCARE:
Support the frightened mother/partner with clear, calm communication throughout
Postpartum/post-cesarean recovery care; neonatal assessment/NICU as indicated
Conduct a team debrief; complete documentation
PREVENTION/AWARENESS (general):

Assess FHR immediately after any rupture of membranes (spontaneous or artificial); heightened vigilance with a high/unengaged presenting part, malpresentation, or polyhydramnios; avoid/cautiously perform AROM when the presenting part is unengaged

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this rupture-of-membranes event?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: A sudden, prolonged fetal bradycardia (or new severe variable decelerations) immediately after rupture of membranes — cord prolapse until proven otherwise; perform a sterile vaginal exam
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — post-ROM bradycardia is the hallmark warning of cord prolapse. RN role: recognize + examine immediately.

opts.text: A palpable, pulsating umbilical cord below the presenting part on vaginal exam (or a visible cord) confirms overt cord prolapse — an obstetric emergency
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms the emergency. RN role: recognize + act immediately.

opts.text: Risk factors present — a high/unengaged presenting part at rupture and a large fluid gush (also malpresentation, polyhydramnios, prematurity, AROM with a high presenting part)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — risk-factor awareness heightens vigilance + explains the event. RN role: recognize high-risk setups.

opts.text: The immediate goal is to RELIEVE CORD COMPRESSION — manually elevate the presenting part off the cord and reposition the mother (knee-chest/Trendelenburg)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — relieving compression restores fetal oxygenation. RN role: manual elevation + positioning.

opts.text: Call for help / activate the obstetric emergency team immediately and lead a coordinated response — do not manage it alone or leave the patient
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — this is a team emergency; leadership/delegation drives the outcome. RN role: activate + lead the team.

opts.text: Support fetal oxygenation + expedite delivery — high-flow maternal O2, IV fluids, STOP oxytocin, continuous FHR monitoring, and prepare for a STAT cesarean
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these measures buy time and move toward definitive delivery. RN role: O2/IV/stop oxytocin/monitor + mobilize cesarean.

opts.text: If the cord is exposed, keep it warm and moist (saline gauze) and minimize handling; have the neonatal team ready for resuscitation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — minimal handling prevents vasospasm; the neonate may need NRP. RN role: protect the cord + ready neonatal team.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; relevant before anesthesia/medications. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Attempt to gently push the prolapsed cord back up into the uterus and then continue with the planned vaginal delivery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Never push the cord back into the uterus, and excessive handling causes umbilical vasospasm (worse hypoxia). With a high presenting part, mobilize a STAT cesarean; relieve compression by elevating the presenting part, not by replacing the cord.

opts.text: Increase the oxytocin to speed up contractions and achieve a faster vaginal delivery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Oxytocin must be STOPPED — contractions intensify cord compression and fetal hypoxia. The path to delivery here is a STAT cesarean, not augmenting labor.

opts.text: Cord prolapse compresses the cord between the presenting part and the pelvis, rapidly impairing fetal oxygenation — an emergency where minutes matter
opts.rat: CORRECT — the pathophysiology + urgency. Foundation for the emergency response.

opts.text: A high/unengaged presenting part at rupture, malpresentation, polyhydramnios, prematurity, and AROM with a high presenting part are risk factors
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk factors. Foundation for vigilance + prevention.

opts.text: The immediate interventions are to relieve cord compression (manual elevation of the presenting part + knee-chest/Trendelenburg), support oxygenation (O2, stop oxytocin), and expedite delivery (STAT cesarean)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the immediate-management framework. Foundation for action.

opts.text: The clinician elevating the presenting part must keep the hand in place (not remove it) until delivery, including during transport to the OR
opts.rat: CORRECT — continuous elevation principle. Foundation for sustained compression relief.

opts.text: An exposed cord should not be pushed back and should be handled minimally and kept warm/moist (handling/cold cause vasospasm); bladder filling is an alternative way to elevate the presenting part
opts.rat: CORRECT — cord-handling + bladder-filling principles. Foundation for protecting cord perfusion.

opts.text: It is a team emergency: instant recognition + calling for help + clear role assignment with closed-loop communication + SBAR + documenting the timeline drive the outcome
opts.rat: CORRECT — leadership/delegation principle. Foundation for a coordinated response.

opts.text: Cord prolapse is best managed by having the mother sit fully upright and walk to encourage the head to descend onto the cord
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Upright/walking would worsen cord compression. Use gravity-relieving positions (knee-chest/Trendelenburg) plus manual elevation of the presenting part OFF the cord.

opts.text: Because the fetal heart rate recovers after the presenting part is elevated, the emergency has resolved and routine labor can simply continue
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. FHR recovery confirms that elevation is working, but the cord is still prolapsed — compression will recur if elevation stops. Maintain elevation and proceed to delivery (STAT cesarean); the emergency is not 'resolved.'

opts.text: Relieve cord compression NOW — manually elevate the presenting part off the cord and reposition the mother (knee-chest/Trendelenburg), keeping the hand in place
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Immediately restoring fetal oxygenation by getting the presenting part off the cord is the first, most time-critical action. RN role: manual elevation + positioning.

opts.text: Call for help / activate and lead the obstetric emergency team (do not leave the patient or manage alone)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Mobilizing the team enables definitive delivery; done simultaneously with compression relief. RN role: activate + lead.

opts.text: Support fetal oxygenation — high-flow maternal O2, IV fluids, STOP oxytocin, continuous FHR monitoring
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. These measures maximize fetal oxygenation while preparing for delivery. RN role: O2/IV/stop oxytocin/monitor.

opts.text: Expedite delivery — prepare for and transfer to a STAT cesarean (maintaining elevation), with the neonatal team ready
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive step. Delivery is the cure; mobilize the OR and neonatal team. RN role: expedite cesarean + neonatal readiness.

opts.text: Support the frightened mother/family and document the timeline; debrief afterward
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + after. Family support + documentation + debrief complete the response. RN role: support + document + debrief.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this cord prolapse.

opts.text: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord with a gloved hand and keep it there until delivery
opts.rat: The core immediate maneuver to relieve cord compression. RN role: continuous manual elevation.

opts.text: Position the mother in knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg to relieve cord pressure
opts.rat: Gravity-relieving positioning reduces compression. RN role: emergency positioning.

opts.text: Call for help / activate the obstetric emergency team immediately and assign clear roles with closed-loop communication
opts.rat: Team activation + leadership are essential. RN role: activate + delegate.

opts.text: Apply high-flow maternal oxygen, give IV fluids, stop oxytocin, and maintain continuous FHR monitoring
opts.rat: Supports fetal oxygenation + reduces compression. RN role: O2/IV/stop oxytocin/monitor.

opts.text: Prepare for and expedite a STAT cesarean delivery with the neonatal team ready for resuscitation
opts.rat: Cesarean is the definitive treatment with a high/unengaged head. RN role: expedite delivery + neonatal readiness.

opts.text: If the cord is exposed, keep it warm and moist with sterile saline gauze and minimize handling
opts.rat: Prevents cord cooling/vasospasm. RN role: protect the cord.

opts.text: Use bladder filling (instill sterile saline via catheter) to elevate the presenting part if continuous manual elevation during transport is impractical
opts.rat: A recognized temporizing maneuver to relieve compression. RN role: alternative elevation method.

opts.text: Push the prolapsed cord back up into the uterus and continue the planned vaginal delivery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — never replace the cord; handling causes vasospasm. With a high presenting part, deliver by STAT cesarean.

opts.text: Increase oxytocin to strengthen contractions and speed a vaginal delivery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — oxytocin must be stopped; contractions worsen cord compression. Move to cesarean.

opts.text: Have the mother sit upright and ambulate to bring the head down onto the cord
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — this worsens cord compression. Use knee-chest/Trendelenburg + manual elevation OFF the cord.

opts.text: Leave the patient alone to go find the physician and gather the team yourself
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — never leave the patient/stop relieving compression; call for help (call bell/overhead/phone) while keeping the presenting part elevated, and delegate others to mobilize the team.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Immediately relieve cord compression — insert a gloved hand and elevate the presenting part off the cord while calling for help (without leaving the patient)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Restoring fetal oxygenation by lifting the presenting part off the cord is the most time-critical action, done while summoning help. RN role: elevate + call out.

opts.text: Reposition the mother (knee-chest/Trendelenburg) and activate/lead the obstetric emergency team with clear role assignments
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Positioning further relieves compression; team activation enables delivery. RN role: position + activate/delegate.

opts.text: Apply high-flow oxygen, give IV fluids, stop oxytocin, and maintain continuous FHR monitoring
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Optimize fetal oxygenation while preparing for delivery. RN role: O2/IV/stop oxytocin/monitor.

opts.text: Expedite transfer to the OR for a STAT cesarean while maintaining manual elevation, with the neonatal team ready for resuscitation
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Move to definitive delivery without releasing the elevation. RN role: expedite cesarean + neonatal readiness.

opts.text: Support the mother/family throughout, document the timeline, and debrief the team afterward
opts.rat: #5 — Throughout/after. Support + documentation + debrief complete the response. RN role: support + document + debrief.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: The nurse recognized post-ROM bradycardia as cord prolapse, manually elevated the presenting part, positioned knee-chest, activated the team, gave O2, and stopped oxytocin — and the FHR recovered toward 110-120
opts.rat: Correct immediate response relieved cord compression and restored fetal oxygenation. Model handling improving fetal status.

opts.text: The clinician maintained continuous manual elevation while transferring the patient to the OR for a STAT cesarean, with the neonatal team ready
opts.rat: Sustained compression relief + expedited definitive delivery. Correct trajectory toward a good outcome.

opts.text: Delivery: the newborn needed brief PPV per NRP then was vigorous (Apgars 5 → 8 → 9); the mother is stable post-cesarean
opts.rat: Good neonatal recovery after rapid recognition + sustained compression relief + timely delivery. Expected favorable outcome.

opts.text: Postpartum: mother recovering well, newborn stable, and the team debriefed and supported the family
opts.rat: Resolution + debrief + family support. Complete, appropriate handling.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the nurse left the patient to go find the physician and did not elevate the presenting part; the fetal bradycardia persisted and worsened during the delay
opts.rat: ⚠️ Leaving the patient + not relieving compression allowed ongoing cord compression and fetal hypoxia. Demonstrates why you call for help WHILE staying + elevating the presenting part.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the cord was pushed back into the uterus and handled repeatedly, and oxytocin was increased to hasten vaginal delivery; the FHR deteriorated further
opts.rat: ⚠️ Replacing/handling the cord caused vasospasm and increasing oxytocin worsened compression. Demonstrates the contraindicated actions in cord prolapse.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the mother was placed upright and encouraged to walk and bear down; the cord compression and bradycardia intensified
opts.rat: ⚠️ Upright/bearing down increases cord compression. Demonstrates why gravity-relieving positions (knee-chest/Trendelenburg) + elevation are used instead.

opts.text: Manual elevation was released once the FHR recovered, on the assumption the emergency was over, before the cesarean; the bradycardia abruptly returned
opts.rat: ⚠️ FHR recovery means elevation is working, not that the prolapse is resolved; releasing the hand restored cord compression. Demonstrates the need to maintain elevation until delivery.

opts.text: In the moments after the presenting part is elevated, the FHR is being continuously monitored and remains stable/improving while the team mobilizes the OR — interventions are correct and ongoing
opts.rat: Stable/improving FHR with elevation maintained while expediting delivery reflects effective ongoing management. Appropriate trajectory.

opts.text: Weeks later, a teaching review notes that assessing the FHR immediately after every rupture of membranes (and cautious AROM only with an engaged presenting part) supports earlier recognition/prevention of cord prolapse
opts.rat: Prevention/awareness practice that improves future recognition and reduces risk. Reflects sound system learning.
